features.summary: Home Depot delivered record Q2 2022 results with sales of $43.8 billion (up 6.5%) and diluted EPS of $5.05 (up 11.5%). Comparable sales increased 5.8% with positive comps across all 19 US regions. The company reaffirmed full-year guidance of approximately 3% sales and comp growth. Pro customers significantly outperformed DIY, with large Pro customers showing particularly strong performance. Digital sales grew 12% and inventory increased $7.2 billion year-over-year, with about half attributed to inflation.
features.management_outlook: Management remains optimistic about long-term home improvement demand fundamentals, citing strong homeowner balance sheets, $8-9 trillion in home equity gains, and aging housing stock. They expect stronger comps in the first half versus second half of 2022. The company is building a comprehensive Pro ecosystem with enhanced supply chain capabilities, with Dallas serving as the most advanced market showing excellent results. Management believes they are well-positioned to gain market share in any economic environment.
features.risks: The company faces multiple macroeconomic crosscurrents including broad-based inflation not seen in four decades, constrained global supply chains, and monetary policy shifts intended to moderate demand. Seasonal categories underperformed expectations in the first half, though management attributes this to weather patterns and tough comparisons rather than fundamental demand weakness. Housing market pressures and potential consumer spending shifts remain key monitoring areas.
features.key_drivers: Pro customer strength is the primary growth driver, with large Pro customers outperforming and healthy project backlogs reported. The company's supply chain investments and Pro ecosystem development are showing strong returns, particularly in Dallas. Digital platform growth of 12% and big-ticket transactions over $1,000 up 11.6% demonstrate continued project demand. Home price appreciation of 30-40% over two years has created substantial homeowner wealth supporting renovation spending.
